text: But the make-believe never lasts. Soon, somebody comes up with another theory, and she’s yanked back into the dark, into whatever haunted house has been built for her this time. The world dissecting the case, the man dissecting her. Betty still can’t see his face, but his hands are always the same. Rough yet meticulous. The callous touch of a stranger. To escape him, she closes her eyes and plays another game in her head. She tries to decide which haunted house she likes best, as though she’s got a choice in it. As though all her silver screen dreams came true, and now she’s a shining star picking a prime piece of real estate in the Hollywood Hills. But the truth is she’d never choose this city. Not anymore, not after what it’s done to her. All she really wants is to run, as fast as she can. If only she could remember how. # The haunted houses keep coming for Betty until there are too many to count. A backyard shed. A motel room. An empty doctor’s office. It seems like any old place could become an abattoir, an ideal locale to butcher a young woman. She wonders if this is how it always goes. Is everywhere just a haunted house in the making? When she can, she breaks free again and wanders a little further, right up to the city limits, her black pumps quivering against the crumbling sidewalk. Yet every time, it’s always the same. She can’t take the last step. She can’t move on from this place. This city of angels, this city of dreams. The whole town is her haunted house, and it won’t let her go. Nobody will let her go. She’s Hollywood’s favorite ghost, after all. And what would we do without our favorite things to keep us company? # Betty tries to learn how to live with it. She tries to learn how to live as a ghost. It’s harder than she expects. Life was always harder for her. She never understood how other people made it look so easy. But she keeps trying anyhow. When the faceless man takes out a bone saw, a kitchen knife, a dull switchblade—it’s different every time because every theory’s got to be different, it’s got to make its own mark—Betty squeezes her eyes closed and pretends she’s merely a magician’s assistant. She pretends it doesn’t hurt. Sometimes, she almost convinces herself it’s true. The years dissolve around her until one day, something twists inside the place Betty’s heart used to be, and she realizes it’s too late. Even if they solved the mystery, even if they knew who did it beyond a shadow of a doubt, it wouldn’t matter now. It’s been too many decades, and the man responsible is long dead. (And let’s face it: it was a man who did it. We don’t need DNA or fingerprints to know that. Some stories can only end one way.) Tonight, the streets are empty, the palmettos drooping in the Los Angeles heat, and Betty goes walking again. Back to the lonely crime scene, that spot where they discovered her. It doesn’t look like much. Just a verdant lawn, a generic patch of grass. There’s nothing to mark that it was hers. Her first haunted house. She gazes down at it, wishing she could feel connected to this place. She wishes she could feel anything. (Is a ghost even supposed to feel, she wonders?) She wishes other people could feel for her too, even though she’s already figured out they don’t. The audience pretends to care, pretends to mourn her, but what everyone really wants is a good show. Betty knows that now. She knows that while the man who murdered her cut her into two pieces, the world is determined to cut her up into even smaller bits than that. Maybe that’s the only way it can go. Because honestly, can you ever honor the dead, ever really do them justice? Or is everybody just a story to sell? (Should we be talking about her now? Should we even speak her name? Or should we just leave a girl to her coffin and call it a day?) # But you can’t help yourself, can you? You’ve been watching her for so long. We all have. The whole world’s never been able to take their eyes off her. She was nobody until she was somebody, the grotesque inverse of the starlet she longed to become. Her greatest role was to play herself, to play dead. (Does that mean we’re all her murderers now? Are we accomplices to what’s become of her?) Even though it’s too late to make a difference, you still want retribution, you want answers. You somehow forget you weren’t the one forced to carry the burden. You’re only a spectator, someone who’s defined Betty through her ending. As if that’s the only moment of her entire life that ever really mattered. (Why is it you feel more kinship to dead girls than living ones? And what do you think that says about you?) The air around you shimmers, and something’s suddenly pulling at you, like fishhooks in your skin. You want to escape it, but you close your eyes instead, and when you open them again, everything’s changed. You’re the girl in the picture now. You’re the one in the basement, the one bisected in the field. No more than a bloodless mannequin, a butchered doll for the world to pick over like vultures. (Is this what you wanted? A vicarious thrill? A chance to know what it feels like to die? Betty could have told you it’s not worth the curiosity, but would you ever bother to listen to her? Will you listen even now?) You try to run, but the haunted houses follow you everywhere you go, the air thick with mildew and heartache, the endless hallways as unforgiving as the dead. This is what she felt, what she’s always
